[PUBLISHED DATE]: July 18th, 2019
[TITLE]: Cut Homeless People In Half by 2025 | Know Your Meme
[METADATA/INTRO]: | Navigation | | About • Origin • Spread • Various Examples • Search Interest • External References • Recent Images |...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: | About • Origin • Spread • Various Examples • Search Interest • External References • Recent Images | 
 Cut Homeless People In Half by 2025 refers to a satirical advertisement created by British street artist Fokawolf which looks like an advertisement from the UK Conservative Party stating "We plan to cut all homeless people in half by 2025." Once the advertisement spread on social media, many who did not know it was a parody began joking as if the UK Conservative Party had intentionally created the ad and misstated their actual plan to cut homelessness in half. This led to image macros and parodies of the advertisement.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2019-07-22
[TITLE]: Did UK Conservatives Vow to 'Cut All Homeless People in Half'? | Snopes.com
[METADATA/INTRO]: In mid-July 2019, social media users began seeing a purported photograph of a sign on public transport bearing the logo of the UK Conservative Party (also known the Conservative and Unionist Party, or simply the "Tories") and bearing a legend...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: In mid-July 2019, social media users began seeing a purported photograph of a sign on public transport bearing the logo of the UK Conservative Party (also known the Conservative and Unionist Party, or simply the "Tories") and bearing a legend reading, "We plan to cut all homeless people in half by 2025": 
 The presumed interpretation of the sign was that the party meant to express they would succeed in addressing the vexing social issue of homelessness to the extent of reducing the number of homeless people by half within six years, but someone phrased that thought poorly and inadvertently produced a hilarious (and cynical) gaffe.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: Hard-hitting posters highlight how homeless people are treated as ‘invisible’ | London Evening Standard | The Standard
[METADATA/INTRO]: The isolation felt by homeless people is being highlighted by hard-hitting posters positioned in spots in a UK city where rough sleepers are likely to rest. The posters bear slogans such as “if this were a homeless person, then most...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: The isolation felt by homeless people is being highlighted by hard-hitting posters positioned in spots in a UK city where rough sleepers are likely to rest. 
 The posters bear slogans such as “if this were a homeless person, then most people wouldn’t even look down” and “a bad place for a poster, an even worse place for a bed”. 
 Set up by the Good Organisation in York, it is hoped they will flag the issue of homelessness, while also advertising the group’s projects to afflicted by it. 
 Kenny Lieske, one of the group’s directors, told the Standard: “It’s often easy to simply walk past homeless people without acknowledging their existence, and that denial is problematic because it leads to a sense of indifference or a feeling that the problem can simply be ignored.”
